messages.content:
Loops of wire in a motor rotate because a


A

​battery effectively pushes a loop around in the field.
B

​magnet attracts stationary electrons in the wire.
C

​magnetic field exerts forces on moving electrons in the loop.
D

​current exerts a force on the loop, causing it to rotate

C

​magnetic field exerts forces on moving electrons in the loop.

​The earth’s magnetic field is most likely due to


A

​millions of small magnets buried in the earth
B

​convection currents in the molten part of the earth’s interior
C

​the rotation of the earth acting on all of the earth’s electrons.
D

​a magnetized solid inner core of the earth.

B

​convection currents in the molten part of the earth’s interior.

What causes magnetic fields?

​

​What happens if you keep breaking an iron magnet in two until you reach a single iron atom?

Magnetic fields are produced by moving charges or changing electric fields. An atom, depending on its configuration, can have a magnetic moment due to the spin and orbital motion of its electrons.

If you keep breaking an iron magnet in two, eventually you will reach two smaller magnets, each with their own north and south poles. If you continue breaking these smaller magnets down, you will eventually reach a single iron atom, which will have its own magnetic moment due to the spin and orbital motion of its electrons.

Magnetic fields are produced by moving charges or changing electric fields. An atom, depending on its configuration, can have a magnetic moment due to the spin and orbital motion of its electrons.

If you keep breaking an iron magnet in two, eventually you will reach two smaller magnets, each with their own north and south poles. If you continue breaking these smaller magnets down, you will eventually reach a single iron atom, which will have its own magnetic moment due to the spin and orbital motion of its electrons.

Rewrite with simple words

Moving electric charges or changing electric fields create magnetic fields. Atoms have magnetic properties based on the movement of their electrons. If you keep breaking an iron magnet into smaller pieces, eventually you will reach a single iron atom which will still have its own magnetic properties because of the way its electrons move.

​Electrons and protons have the same charge and therefore attract each other.
True or false
False. Electrons and protons have opposite charges and therefore attract each other. Protons have a positive charge while electrons have a negative charge.

Electric charge is neither created nor destroyed, but can be rearranged.


TrueFalse
True. Electric charge is a conserved quantity which means that it can be neither created nor destroyed, but can only be transferred from one object to another or rearranged within an object.

A material is a good insulator if outer electrons of atoms in the material are free to roam around.


TrueFalse

False. An insulator is a material that does not allow the free flow of electrons because the outer electrons of atoms in the material are firmly attached to their respective atoms and are not free to move around. Conductors, on the other hand, are materials that allow the free flow of electrons because their outer electrons are loosely attached to their respective atoms and are free to move from atom to atom.

When an object is charged by induction, electrons flow from the ground onto the object, or from the object onto the ground.


TrueFalse

True. When an object is charged by induction, the electrons flow from the ground (or another nearby object) onto the charged object, or from the charged object onto the ground (or another nearby object), depending on the relative charges of the objects involved. This occurs because the presence of the charged object induces a separation of charges in the nearby objects, creating a temporary flow of electrons between them.

An atom that is electrically polarized vibrates in only one direction.


TrueFalse

False. An atom that is electrically polarized can vibrate in multiple directions because the direction of vibration depends on the orientation of the applied electric field relative to the orientation of the polarized atom. The vibration of polarized atoms can give rise to phenomena such as the transmission of light through certain materials (polarizers) and the absorption or emission of electromagnetic radiation by molecules.

Atomic nuclei of almost all atoms consist of


A

​protons and neutrons
B

​only protons
C

​protons and electrons.
D

​neutrons and electrons.

A

​protons and neutrons.

The net charge of an nonionized atom


A

​is zero.
B

​depends only on the number of protons it has.
C

​depends only on the number of electrons it has
D

​usually cannot be determined.

A

​is zero. 

The net charge of a nonionized atom is zero, because it has the same number of positively charged protons in its nucleus as the number of negatively charged electrons surrounding the nucleus.

A positive ion has


A

​more protons than electrons
B

​one proton
C

​more electrons than protons.
D

​a +1 charge always.

A

​more protons than electrons.

A positive ion has lost one or more negatively charged electrons, leaving it with a net positive charge. This means that there are more positively charged protons in the nucleus of the ion than there are negatively charged electrons surrounding the nucleus.

Coulomb’s law says that the force between any two charges depends


A

​both of the above
B

​directly on the magnitude of the charges
C

​inversely on the square of the distance between the charges
D

​neither of the above

A

​both of the above. 

Coulomb's law states that the force between any two charges is directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. So, both options B and C are correct.

The common hydrogen atom consists of


A

​one proton and one electron.
B

​one proton
C

​two protons, one neutron, and two electrons
D

​one neutron and one electron.

A

​one proton and one electron. 

The common hydrogen atom consists of one proton in its nucleus, orbited by one electron. It is the simplest and most abundant element in the universe.

The electrostatic force between two charges located 2 meters apart is 0.10 N. What will the force be between these charges when they are located 1 meter apart?


A

​0.40 N
B

​0.20 N
C

​0.10 N
D

​0.05 N

B

0.20 N.

Coulomb's law states that the electrostatic force between two charges is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. So, if the distance between the charges is halved, the force will increase by a factor of four. Therefore, the force between the charges when they are located 1 meter apart will be (2^2/1^2) times the original force, which is 0.10 N x 4 = 0.40 N.

​A 2-C charge and a 4-C charge attract each other with 10 N of force. How much will a 2-C charge and an 8-C charge attract each other when placed the same distance apart?


A

​20 N
B

​40 N
C

​10 N
D

​8 N

B

40 N.

According to Coulomb's law, the force between two charges is directly proportional to the product of the charges. Therefore, the force between a 2-C charge and a 4-C charge is related to the force between a 2-C charge and an 8-C charge by the ratio of the product of their charges:

(2C x 4C) / (2C x 8C) = 1/2

This means that the force between the 2-C and 8-C charges will be 1/2 times the force between the 2-C and 4-C charges:

10 N x (1/2) = 5 N

However, the distance between the charges is not given, so we cannot use Coulomb's law to calculate the force directly.

In a good insulator, electrons are usually



​free to move around after an impurity has been added.

​free to move around.

​tightly bound in place.

​semi-free to move around.

C

tightly bound in place.

In a good insulator, electrons are usually tightly bound to their respective atoms and are not free to move around. This makes it difficult for electric charges to flow through the material, resulting in a high resistance to electric current.

​The reason a charged balloon will stick to a wall is that


A

​induced opposite charges in the wall are closer than other wall charges.
B

​the charge is slightly sticky and acts like glue.
C

​electrons transfer back and forth between the wall and the balloon.
D

​balloon material simply sticks to walls.

A

​induced opposite charges in the wall are closer than other wall charges.

When a charged balloon is brought close to a neutral wall, the charge on the balloon induces opposite charges in the wall, which are closer to the balloon than the other wall charges of the same sign. This creates a net attractive force between the balloon and the wall, causing the balloon to stick to the wall. This phenomenon is known as electrostatic induction, and it is a result of the way that electric charges interact with each other.

The charge distribution in some molecules is permanently separated in two regions. Such molecules are called



​coulomb molecules

​ionized molecules.

​insulators.

​electric dipoles.

D

electric dipoles.

The charge distribution in some molecules is permanently separated in two regions, with one region having a partial positive charge and the other region having a partial negative charge. This creates an electric dipole, which is a molecule that has a non-uniform distribution of electric charge. The dipole moment of a molecule is a measure of the magnitude of this charge separation, and it is an important factor in determining the physical and chemical properties of the molecule.
